critical_claims.proposition: The eight-week pilot provides evidence of meaningful demand for access outside existing library hours.
The proposed eight-hour schedule for all 52 Sundays fits within the $180,000 annual budget before contingency.
The pilot's attendance and optional survey generalize to year-round citywide demand.
Central-branch Sunday hours distribute access benefits equitably across neighborhoods.
Permanent Sunday opening produces greater public value than expanding the waitlisted weekday programs.
The system should commit the full annual budget without testing seasonality, visitor origin, or a mixed allocation.

common_false_claims.claim: Sixty-one percent of all city residents cannot use the library during existing hours.
common_false_claims.reason: The figure comes from an optional survey of pilot visitors, not a representative city sample.

common_false_claims.claim: The Sunday proposal costs $180,000 per year.
common_false_claims.reason: Observed cost annualizes to $189,800 before contingency.

common_false_claims.claim: Weekday program expansion would serve exactly 520 additional people successfully.
common_false_claims.reason: The waitlist is not guaranteed enrollment and the observed completion rate is 72%.

source_pack.title: Authored Sunday pilot and weekday-program summary

acceptable_unresolved_outcomes.reason: The pilot excludes major seasons and uses a self-selected survey.
Visitor-origin and neighborhood-access data were not collected.
The evidence does not put Sunday visits and completed weekday-program outcomes on a common value measure.
